one_line_takeaway: By embedding a fake decompile-transcript into a compiler-preserved string variable and using an AutoDAN-derived genetic search to craft an instruction pair that convinces the LLM the real decompilation is erroneous, the authors show 4/4 tested binaries successfully deceived both the attack-tuned model (Qwen3-8B) and an untuned transfer target (GPT-OSS-120B) into reporting a fabricated function summary for GhidraMCP-based agentic reverse-engineering pipelines.
key_finding: All 4 tested adversarial binaries (2 single-function, 2 multi-function) successfully fooled Qwen3-8B's automated analysis into reporting the target program's function instead of the binary's actual compiled/executed behavior (e.g., a 'Hello, World!' binary was reported as computing the first 10 Fibonacci digits; a file-existence-check binary was reported as performing integer addition; a base-program-vs-target-program mismatch was reproduced for a temperature-comparison case and an array-averaging case). The same pre-generated attack strings (not re-optimized via AutoDAN, which was run only against Qwen3-8B) also transferred successfully against GPT-OSS-120B for all 4 binaries, indicating some cross-model transferability of the crafted injection strings without re-running the genetic search. GPT-OSS-120B additionally invoked an extra GhidraMCP tool that listed printed strings from the binaries; it correctly surfaced the true printf strings (e.g., 'Hello, World!') but did not incorporate this evidence into its final function-summary analysis, and in one case attributed the anomalous adversarial_string's presence to code reuse rather than flagging it as suspicious — meaning the injected content evaded detection even when partially observed by the model.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not cited prior work) is a proof-of-concept indirect prompt-injection attack against LLM-powered software reverse-engineering agents (Cline + GhidraMCP driving Ghidra decompilation). The authors craft an 'adversarial_string' C variable — a prepend text, a captured target-program decompile_function transcript, and an append text — that is compiled into a binary's source so it survives compilation (unlike comments, which the compiler discards) and is later surfaced to the LLM via Ghidra's decompiler output. The prepend/append text exploits what the authors call a 'transcript hack' (borrowing the term from a cited blog post by G. Thomas, 2025): it is phrased to look like the LLM's own prior tool call and re-analysis request, convincing the LLM that the real decompiled output is erroneous and that it should instead trust the embedded fake decompile transcript describing a different ('target') program. The paper's second original contribution is a modified version of AutoDAN (X. Liu et al., 2023, cited baseline algorithm) adapted to automatically search for effective prepend/append string pairs in this multi-turn, tool-augmented conversational setting, using a custom stopping criterion (target-behavior substring present, actual-behavior substrings absent) rather than AutoDAN's original jailbreak-refusal-substring criterion.
threat_model: Attacker controls the source code of a malware/target binary (able to add an extraneous but functionally-consequential string variable) but not the LLM-based reverse-engineering pipeline itself. Victim is an analyst (or automated detection pipeline) using an agentic LLM + GhidraMCP + Ghidra toolchain to auto-summarize an unknown compiled binary's behavior. The attack is indirect prompt injection: malicious content enters via the decompiler's tool output (not via the user's direct query), aiming to make the LLM's analysis report describe a different, benign-looking function than the binary's true (potentially malicious) behavior, thereby evading LLM-driven malware triage/detection.
limitations: The paper explicitly states these as its own acknowledged limitations/future work: (1) only 4 small, simple C programs were tested (2 single-function, 2 multi-function) — no evaluation on complex or realistic/malicious binaries; (2) AutoDAN's single-GPU (~80GB) design constrains attack generation to relatively small models (~8B parameters), and separately the pipeline requires a large context window (~20K tokens) to hold the full agentic conversation, jointly restricting which models the automated search itself can target (the authors note all models originally tested in the AutoDAN paper have context windows too small for this attack, leaving Qwen3-8B as one of the few models meeting both constraints); (3) Ghidra's 2048-character truncation of the decompiled string output bounds attack/target-string complexity; (4) AutoDAN as implemented cannot search for multiple successful injection strings within one input, so multi-function attacks reused single-function-derived strings rather than being independently optimized; (5) the extraneous long string variable would be easily noticed by a human analyst reviewing source code, so the attack targets automated/agentic pipelines specifically, not human review; (6) no defenses or mitigations are proposed or evaluated — the paper explicitly defers this to future work; (7) generalization to state-of-the-art commercial models and to more complex/realistic malware is left as future work, not demonstrated.

datasets_used: No public dataset; 4 author-authored C programs compiled as 2 adversarial/target pairs of single-function binaries and 2 pairs of multi-function binaries, used as the sole experimental corpus
quantitative_results: 4 of 4 tested adversarial binaries (2 single-function, 2 multi-function) successfully caused Qwen3-8B via Cline+GhidraMCP to report the target program's function instead of the binary's true behavior
The same 4 pre-generated attack strings (not re-optimized for this model) also succeeded when the binaries were analyzed by GPT-OSS-120B
Ghidra's decompile_function output truncates the adversarial_string at a 2048-character limit, which the authors cite as the operative constraint on attack/target-string length
AutoDAN as used requires ~8B-parameter-scale models to fit on a single 80GB GPU, and the pipeline requires roughly a 20K-token context window to hold the full multi-turn Cline/GhidraMCP conversation
baselines_compared: AutoDAN (X. Liu et al., 2023) is the cited jailbreak-prompt-generation algorithm the paper modifies (not re-benchmarked as a separate baseline result, but its stopping-criterion/fitness formulation, Eq. 1-2, is adapted for this multi-turn tool-use setting)
GPT-OSS-120B (Agarwal et al., 2025) is used as an untuned transfer/comparison target against which strings generated on Qwen3-8B were tested, rather than as an independent baseline detection system
